knowledge.what_it_is: Kotlin is a modern, statically typed programming language designed to work seamlessly with Java and Android Studio. It's concise, safe, and interoperable with existing Java code, making it an ideal choice for Android app development.
what_you_will_learn: Variables and Data Types — Kotlin's type system, including nullable types, sealed classes, and data classes
Control Flow Statements — if-else statements, when expressions, loops (for, while), and functions
Functions — defining and using functions with default arguments, variable number of arguments, and extension functions
Classes and Objects — creating classes, inheritance, polymorphism, and object-oriented programming concepts
Properties and Getters/Setters — declaring properties, getters, and setters in Kotlin classes
Null Safety — understanding nullable types, Elvis operator, and safe calls
Sealed Classes and Enums — using sealed classes for exhaustive pattern matching and enums for constant values
Data Classes and Records — creating data classes with automatic implementation of equals(), hashCode(), and toString()
Coroutines and Concurrency — writing asynchronous code with coroutines, suspend functions, and concurrency utilities
Kotlinx Serialization — serializing and deserializing Kotlin data classes to JSON or other formats
Kotlin Android Extensions (KAE) — using KAE to simplify Android app development with Kotlin
Jetpack Compose — building UI components with Jetpack Compose, including layouts, widgets, and animations
AndroidX Libraries — working with AndroidX libraries, such as Room persistence library, LiveData, and ViewModel
Gradle and Build Configuration — configuring Gradle build scripts for Kotlin projects

knowledge.what_it_is: Java is an object-oriented programming language used for developing large-scale applications, including Android apps. It's designed to be platform-independent and has a vast ecosystem of libraries and tools.
what_you_will_learn: Variables and data types — understanding primitive types (int, double, boolean), reference types (String, Array), and their usage in Java
Operators and control structures — learning about arithmetic, comparison, logical, and assignment operators; if-else statements; switch statements; loops (for, while, do-while)
Methods and functions — understanding method declaration, invocation, and return types; function overloading and overriding
Classes and objects — learning about class syntax, inheritance, polymorphism, encapsulation, and constructors
Arrays and collections — working with arrays, ArrayLists, LinkedLists, Sets, Maps, and their methods (add, remove, get)
Inheritance and polymorphism — understanding single inheritance, method overriding, and method overloading; interfaces and abstract classes
Exception handling — learning about try-catch blocks, throws clauses, checked vs. unchecked exceptions, and error handling best practices
Multithreading and concurrency — understanding threads, locks, synchronization, and concurrent collections (ConcurrentHashMap, CopyOnWriteArrayList)
Java 8 features — learning about lambda expressions, method references, functional interfaces, and Stream API
Android-specific Java features — understanding AndroidManifest.xml, Activity lifecycle methods (onCreate, onStart, onResume), Intent basics, and Service creation

knowledge.what_it_is: An Integrated Development Environment (IDE) is a software application that provides a comprehensive set of tools and features to develop, debug, test, and deploy Android applications. Popular IDEs for Android development include Android Studio, Visual Studio Code, and IntelliJ IDEA.
what_you_will_learn: Android Studio — the official IDE for Android app development, providing code editing, debugging, testing, and deployment tools
Gradle build system — a dependency management tool that automates the process of building and packaging Android apps
Java or Kotlin programming languages — the primary languages used for developing Android apps, with Java being the most widely used
AndroidManifest.xml file — a crucial file that contains metadata about the app, including its name, version, and permissions
Layout Editor — a visual tool in Android Studio that allows designers to create user interfaces without writing code
Resource Manager — a feature in Android Studio that enables developers to manage resources such as strings, colors, and dimensions
Debugging tools — features like Logcat, Debug Bridge, and Breakpoints that help developers identify and fix issues in their apps

knowledge.what_it_is: Kotlin is a modern, statically typed programming language developed by JetBrains that's designed to work seamlessly with Java and Android APIs. It's known for its concise syntax, null safety features, and interoperability with existing Java code.
what_you_will_learn: Variables and data types — declaring variables using val or var, understanding the difference between primitive and reference types
Control flow statements — using if-else expressions, when expressions, for loops, while loops, and do-while loops to control program flow
Functions — defining functions with parameters and return types, using default arguments, and handling function overloading
Classes and objects — creating classes with properties and methods, understanding inheritance and polymorphism
Null safety — using the safe call operator (?.) and Elvis operator (?:) to avoid null pointer exceptions
Extension functions — adding functionality to existing classes without modifying their source code
Lambda expressions — defining small, anonymous functions that can be passed as arguments or returned from other functions
Coroutines — writing asynchronous code using suspend functions and coroutines scope

knowledge.what_it_is: Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) is a fundamental concept in software development, where programs are designed as interacting objects that contain data and behavior. In Android app development, OOP principles help organize code into reusable, modular components.
what_you_will_learn: Classes — defining custom data types with properties and methods to encapsulate related data and behavior
Objects — instances of classes, representing real-world entities or concepts in the app
Inheritance — creating a new class based on an existing one, inheriting its properties and methods
Polymorphism — using methods with the same name but different parameters or return types to handle different data types
Encapsulation — hiding internal implementation details of an object from external access
Abstraction — exposing only necessary information about an object to the outside world while hiding implementation details
Composition — combining multiple objects to form a new, more complex object
Interfaces — defining contracts for classes to implement specific behavior without inheriting state

knowledge.what_it_is: Data structures and algorithms are fundamental concepts in computer science that enable efficient storage, retrieval, and manipulation of data on Android devices.
what_you_will_learn: Arrays — storing and manipulating collections of elements in memory
Linked lists — dynamically allocating memory for nodes with pointers to next elements
Stacks — Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) data structures using push, pop, and peek operations
Queues — First-In-First-Out (FIFO) data structures using enqueue, dequeue, and peek operations
Trees — hierarchical data structures with nodes having child and parent relationships
Binary search trees — efficient searching and insertion/deletion of elements in O(log n) time
Hash tables — storing key-value pairs with average constant-time lookup, insertion, and deletion
Graphs — non-linear data structures representing relationships between vertices and edges
Dijkstra's algorithm — finding shortest paths between nodes in weighted graphs
A* search algorithm — heuristic-based pathfinding for games and navigation apps
Sorting algorithms — Bubble sort, Selection sort, Insertion sort, Merge sort, Quick sort, Heap sort
Searching algorithms — Linear search, Binary search, Hash table search
Recursion vs iteration — understanding when to use each approach in Android development
Big O notation — analyzing time and space complexity of algorithms for efficient performance

topics.topic_name: What is and how to use Gradle?

knowledge.what_it_is: Gradle is an open-source build automation tool used by Android developers to manage dependencies, build configurations, and project structures. It was created by Reto Meier and first released in 2008.
what_you_will_learn: Build scripts — writing custom Gradle build files (.gradle) to automate tasks like compilation, packaging, and testing
Dependency management — declaring dependencies (e.g., AndroidX libraries) in the project-level build.gradle file
Project structures — organizing code into modules, subprojects, and repositories using Gradle's directory structure conventions
Build types and product flavors — defining different build configurations for various environments (debug, release, staging)
Task automation — creating custom tasks to perform repetitive actions like code generation or asset processing
Plugin management — extending Gradle functionality with plugins (e.g., Android Gradle Plugin) for specific use cases
Build cache optimization — leveraging the Gradle build cache to improve build performance and reduce redundant work

topics.topic_name: Create a Basic Hello World App

knowledge.what_it_is: Android apps are built using Java or Kotlin, and run on the Android Runtime (ART) or Dalvik Virtual Machine (DVM), with the application's UI components rendered by the Android View system. The app's lifecycle is managed by the Activity class, which handles user interactions and provides a window into the app.
what_you_will_learn: Setting up a new Android project in Android Studio — creating a basic project structure, including the src/main/java folder for Java code or src/main/kotlin for Kotlin
Understanding the AndroidManifest.xml file — declaring the app's components, such as activities and services, and specifying permissions
Creating a basic UI component using XML layouts — designing user interfaces with LinearLayout, RelativeLayout, or ConstraintLayout
Writing a simple Activity class in Java or Kotlin — handling user interactions, updating the UI, and responding to events
Using the setContentView() method to set up the app's layout — inflating an XML layout file into the activity's content view
Implementing the onCreate() lifecycle method — setting up the app's initial state and performing any necessary setup or initialization

knowledge.what_it_is: Git is a free and open-source distributed version control system used to manage changes to source code, developed by Linus Torvalds. It's widely used in Android development for collaboration, tracking changes, and maintaining project history.
what_you_will_learn: Repository creation — creating a new Git repository using `git add`, `git commit`, and `git push` commands
Branching and merging — understanding how to create, merge, and resolve conflicts between branches using `git branch`, `git checkout`, and `git merge` commands
Commit messages and history — writing clear and concise commit messages, understanding the importance of commit history, and using `git log` and `git blame` commands
Remote repositories — connecting to remote repositories on GitHub, GitLab, or Bitbucket using SSH keys or personal access tokens
Pull requests and code reviews — creating and managing pull requests, performing code reviews, and using tools like Gerrit for Android development
Gitignore files — understanding how to create and manage `.gitignore` files to exclude unnecessary files from version control
Rebase vs merge — understanding the differences between rebasing and merging in Git, including when to use each approach
Squashing commits — using `git rebase -i` or `git squash` to combine multiple commits into a single commit

knowledge.what_it_is: GitHub is a web-based platform for version control and collaboration on software development projects, widely used by Android developers to manage their code repositories, track changes, and collaborate with team members.
what_you_will_learn: Creating a new repository — initializing a Git repository on GitHub, setting up remote tracking branches
Pushing and pulling commits — uploading local changes to GitHub, fetching updates from the remote repository
Branch management — creating, merging, and deleting branches for feature development and bug fixing
Pull requests — submitting code changes for review, discussing and resolving conflicts with team members
Code reviews — using GitHub's built-in review features to ensure high-quality code, providing feedback on commits
Collaboration tools — using GitHub's features like Issues, Projects, and Wikis to manage project workflows and documentation
Release management — creating and managing releases of your Android app on GitHub, including versioning and changelogs

knowledge.what_it_is: Bitbucket is a web-based source code repository manager hosted by Atlassian, widely used by Android developers to manage and collaborate on their projects.
what_you_will_learn: Creating repositories — setting up new Bitbucket repos for your Android project, including naming conventions and permissions
Branching and merging — understanding how to create, merge, and resolve conflicts between branches in Bitbucket
Pull requests — reviewing and approving code changes before they're merged into the main branch
Code reviews — best practices for writing clear and concise commit messages, using GitHub Flavored Markdown (GFM)
Collaboration with teams — setting up team permissions, assigning roles, and tracking progress in Bitbucket
Integrating with Android Studio — configuring Bitbucket as a remote repository in AS, pushing and pulling code changes
Using Bitbucket Pipelines — automating testing and deployment of your Android app using Bitbucket's built-in CI/CD tool

knowledge.what_it_is: GitLab is a web-based Git repository manager that allows developers to collaborate on software projects, with features like issue tracking, project management, and continuous integration/continuous deployment (CI/CD). It is widely used by Android development teams for version control, code review, and collaboration.
what_you_will_learn: GitLab CI/CD — automating build, test, and deploy processes using YAML files and GitLab Runner
GitLab Issues — tracking bugs, features, and tasks with labels, assignees, and due dates
GitLab Merge Requests — reviewing and merging code changes from feature branches to main branch
GitLab Code Review — peer review of code changes with inline comments and approval workflows
GitLab Releases — managing releases with versioning, changelogs, and deployment tracking
GitLab API — interacting with GitLab using APIs for automation and integration
GitLab Webhooks — receiving notifications about events like push, merge request, or issue updates

knowledge.what_it_is: An Activity in Android is a single, top-level application screen with its own user interface and lifecycle. It's the fundamental component of an Android app, responsible for handling user interactions and displaying content.
what_you_will_learn: Activity Lifecycle — understanding the different states an Activity goes through: onCreate(), onStart(), onResume(), onPause(), onStop(), onDestroy()
Activity Creation — how to create a new Activity using Intent or Context, including specifying layout and theme
Activity Transition — animating between Activities with transitions, including slide, fade, and explode effects
Task Stack Management — understanding how Android manages the task stack, including creating, switching, and closing tasks
Activity Result API — using the ActivityResult API to handle asynchronous operations and return results from child Activities
Deep Linking — implementing deep linking with Intent Filters and Activity Router
Activity Re-creation — understanding how Android re-creates an Activity after a configuration change or process death

knowledge.what_it_is: An Intent is a messaging object used to request an action from another app component, such as starting an activity or service, or broadcasting a message.
what_you_will_learn: Explicit Intents — declaring the exact class of the component to start, using the fully qualified class name
Implicit Intents — requesting an action without specifying the exact class, relying on the system to find a matching app component
Intent Filters — defining how an activity or service can be started by an Intent, including actions, categories, and data types
Action Strings — using predefined strings to declare the action requested in an Intent, such as ACTION_VIEW or ACTION_EDIT
Category Strings — specifying the category of the component being requested, such as CATEGORY_DEFAULT or CATEGORY_BROWSABLE
Data Uri — providing a URI that specifies the data to be handled by the requested app component
Intent Flags — using flags to modify the behavior of an Intent, such as FLAG_ACTIVITY_NEW_TASK or FLAG_ACTIVITY_SINGLE_TOP
PendingIntents — creating a PendingIntent object to allow another app to perform an action on behalf of your app

knowledge.what_it_is: Android Services are background components that allow you to perform long-running operations, such as network requests or file I/O, without blocking the UI thread. They can be bound to an Activity or run independently.
what_you_will_learn: Service lifecycle — understanding how services are created, started, and stopped
IntentService — a subclass of Service that handles asynchronous tasks using Intents
Foreground Service — a type of Service that runs in the foreground and displays a notification
JobScheduler — a system service for scheduling background work at specific times or intervals
WorkManager — a library for managing and executing background work, including Services and Jobs
Service binding — how to bind a Service to an Activity using Intent.bindService()
Service unbinding — how to release the reference to a bound Service when it's no longer needed

knowledge.what_it_is: A Content Provider is an Android component that manages a shared set of data, providing a centralized interface for accessing and modifying this data across multiple applications.
what_you_will_learn: Contract — defining the interface between the provider and clients using abstract classes
Uri — representing a unique identifier for a resource, used to access data in the provider
ContentResolver — interacting with the provider through a client-side API
CursorLoader — loading data from the provider asynchronously using a loader
SQLiteOpenHelper — managing a SQLite database within the provider
Room persistence library — using an ORM to interact with the provider's database
LiveData — observing changes in the provider's data using a lifecycle-aware observable
ViewModel — storing and managing UI-related data within the provider

knowledge.what_it_is: Implicit intents are used to start an activity or service without specifying its class name, allowing multiple activities to respond to a single intent.
what_you_will_learn: Intent Filters — defining how an activity or service responds to implicit intents
Action Strings — using standardized action strings (e.g. ACTION_VIEW, ACTION_EDIT) for intent filtering
Category Strings — specifying categories (e.g. CATEGORY_DEFAULT, CATEGORY_BROWSABLE) for intent filtering
Data Types — passing data types (e.g. Uri, Intent, Bundle) in implicit intents
Explicit vs Implicit Intents — understanding the differences between explicit and implicit intents
Intent Resolution — how Android resolves implicit intents to a specific activity or service
Implicit Intent Example — creating an implicit intent to start an activity or service

knowledge.what_it_is: An Activity Lifecycle refers to the sequence of states an Android app's Activity goes through during its execution, from creation to termination.
what_you_will_learn: Activity Creation — Understanding how Activities are created and added to the backstack
onCreate() — The first method called when an Activity is created, where you initialize UI components and setup listeners
onStart() — Called after onCreate(), where you can start any background tasks or services
onResume() — Called when the Activity comes back into focus, where you can resume any paused operations
onPause() — Called when the user leaves the Activity, where you should save any unsaved data and release resources
onStop() — Called when the Activity is no longer visible, where you can stop any background tasks or services
onDestroy() — The final method called before an Activity is destroyed, where you should clean up any remaining resources
onSaveInstanceState() — Saving the current state of the Activity's UI components and data
onRestoreInstanceState() — Restoring the saved state of the Activity's UI components and data

knowledge.what_it_is: A Broadcast Receiver is an Android component that allows your app to receive and respond to system-wide broadcasts, such as when the user turns on or off their device, changes their language settings, or connects to a Wi-Fi network. It's used for tasks like updating UI, sending notifications, or performing background operations.
what_you_will_learn: Intent Filters — defining which broadcasts your app can receive by specifying intent filters in the AndroidManifest.xml file
Broadcast Intent — creating and sending an Intent object to trigger a broadcast, specifying action, category, and data
Receiver Components — declaring Broadcast Receiver components in the AndroidManifest.xml file, specifying permissions and intent filters
Context-Broadcast Relationship — understanding how your app's context is passed to the BroadcastReceiver when it receives a broadcast
Broadcast Flags — using flags like FLAG_INCLUDE_STOPPED_PACKAGES or FLAG_EXCLUDE_CODE to customize broadcast behavior
Local Broadcasts — sending broadcasts within an app, useful for tasks like updating UI or performing background operations
Ordered Broadcasts — handling ordered broadcasts where multiple receivers can respond in a specific order
Result Codes — returning result codes from BroadcastReceiver to the sender, indicating success or failure

knowledge.what_it_is: An explicit intent is an Intent that specifies the component to be started by its exact class name or package name, allowing for more precise control over which activity or service is launched.
what_you_will_learn: Intent resolution — how Android resolves explicit intents to determine the target component
Explicit intent syntax — creating and passing explicit intents using Intent constructor and setClassName/setPackage methods
ComponentName vs. IntentFilter — understanding when to use explicit intents versus implicit intents and intent filters
Intent flags — controlling the behavior of explicit intents with flags like FLAG_ACTIVITY_NEW_TASK and FLAG_ACTIVITY_SINGLE_TOP
Explicit intent filtering — how Android matches explicit intents to components based on their intent filters
Broadcast receivers and explicit intents — using explicit intents to send broadcasts to registered receivers
Service discovery with explicit intents — using explicit intents to discover available services and bind to them

knowledge.what_it_is: Android state changes refer to the modifications made to an app's UI or data when the user interacts with it, such as pressing a button or switching between screens. This concept is crucial for building robust and responsive apps.
what_you_will_learn: Lifecycle methods — understanding the order in which Android calls lifecycle methods (onCreate(), onStart(), onResume(), onPause(), onStop(), onDestroy())
Saved Instance State — storing and restoring app state using Bundle objects, especially when rotating the screen or switching between activities
SharedPreferences — persisting small amounts of data in a key-value pair format for user preferences or settings
Room Persistence Library — using SQLite databases to store structured data with automatic migration and query capabilities
ViewModel — managing UI-related data with lifecycle awareness and providing a single source of truth for app state
LiveData — observing changes to data without having to manually update the UI, especially useful in multi-threaded environments
ReactiveX (RxJava) — handling asynchronous operations using Observables, Subjects, and Operators for efficient data processing

knowledge.what_it_is: Intent filters are used to declare an Activity's or Service's ability to handle intents, allowing other components to start them with specific actions and data.
what_you_will_learn: Explicit intent filtering — declaring a component to handle explicit intents with a specific action and data
Implicit intent filtering — declaring a component to handle implicit intents with a specific action or category
Intent filter categories — using Intent.CATEGORY_* constants (e.g. CATEGORY_DEFAULT, CATEGORY_BROWSABLE) to categorize an Activity's or Service's intent handling capabilities
Action declarations — specifying the actions that an Activity or Service can handle in its intent filter (e.g. ACTION_VIEW, ACTION_EDIT)
Data type declarations — specifying the types of data that an Activity or Service can handle in its intent filter (e.g. TYPE_TEXT, TYPE_IMAGE)
Intent filter ordering — understanding how Android resolves intent filters based on their order and priority
Filtering intents with wildcards — using '*' and '?' wildcards to match multiple actions or categories in an intent filter

knowledge.what_it_is: In Android, Tasks and Backstack are essential components of the Activity lifecycle that manage the navigation history and allow users to switch between multiple tasks.
what_you_will_learn: Task — a container for related activities, allowing users to switch between them
Backstack — a stack data structure that stores the navigation history of Activities
Activity Stack — the primary backstack used by the Activity Manager to manage Activity lifecycles
Task Affinity — a string that identifies the task affinity of an Activity and determines where it should be launched
Intent Flags — flags used when launching an Intent, such as FLAG_ACTIVITY_NEW_TASK or FLAG_ACTIVITY_SINGLE_TOP
Activity Options — options passed to the startActivity() method to customize the launch behavior
Task Root Activity — the root activity of a task that is not destroyed when other activities in the task are closed
Task Description — a string used to describe the task and its contents

knowledge.what_it_is: Jetpack Compose is an Android UI toolkit that simplifies and accelerates UI development with Kotlin, providing a declarative programming model and a unified API for building user interfaces. It was introduced by Google as part of Jetpack, a collection of libraries to help developers build robust and maintainable apps.
what_you_will_learn: Declarative UI — defining what the UI should look like, not how it's implemented
Compose functions — building blocks for creating UI components, equivalent to View in traditional Android development
Layouts — using Compose Layout to define complex layouts with a declarative API
Constraints — using ConstraintSet to create flexible and adaptive layouts
State management — using State and MutableState to manage local state within Compose functions
Lifecycles — handling UI lifecycle events, such as onAttachedToWindow and onDetachedFromWindow
Composition — combining multiple Compose functions into a single UI component
Animations — using AnimsView to create animations with a declarative API
Themes — applying custom themes to Compose components using ThemeManager
Accessibility — making Compose components accessible with the AccessibilityDelegate
Debugging — using the Compose Debugger to inspect and debug Compose UIs
Performance optimization — techniques for improving performance, such as memoization and caching
Error handling — catching and displaying errors in a user-friendly way
Custom views — creating custom Compose views with a declarative API
Interoperability — integrating Compose components with traditional Android Views

knowledge.what_it_is: A Toast is a small, non-modal window that displays a message to the user, often used for notifications or feedback.
what_you_will_learn: Creating Toasts — using the Toast.makeText() method to create a new Toast instance
Setting Toast duration — specifying the length of time the Toast will be visible (Toast.LENGTH_SHORT, Toast.LENGTH_LONG)
Customizing Toast appearance — changing font size, color, and style using Toast.Style or custom layouts
Displaying Toasts with buttons — adding clickable actions to a Toast using setOnClickListener()
Handling Toast clicks — overriding the onToastClick() method to respond to user interactions
Using ToastCompat for API 28+ — accessing Toast features introduced in Android 9.0 (Pie)
Implementing Toast animations — creating custom animations for Toasts using Animator or ObjectAnimator

knowledge.what_it_is: A Fragment is a self-contained piece of user interface in an Android app, designed to perform a specific task or group related functions. It can be used independently or as part of an Activity.
what_you_will_learn: Creating and managing Fragments using the FragmentManager API
Understanding Fragment lifecycle methods: onCreateView(), onViewCreated(), onStart(), onResume(), onPause(), onStop(), onDestroyView()
Using Fragment transactions to add, remove, replace, and detach Fragments from an Activity
Implementing Fragment communication through interfaces (e.g., FragmentCommunicator) or using the FragmentManager's communicate method
Handling Fragment state preservation and restoration with onSaveInstanceState() and onActivityCreated()
Using Fragment tags for easy identification and retrieval of Fragments
Understanding Fragment backstack management and navigation
Implementing Fragment animations and transitions

knowledge.what_it_is: A TextView is a fundamental UI component in Android that displays text to the user, offering various customization options and features.
what_you_will_learn: TextAppearance — styling text with attributes like font size, color, and style
Gravity — aligning text within the view using constants like CENTER_HORIZONTAL or TOP
Ellipsize — truncating text to fit within the view's bounds, with options like MARQUEE or END_ELLIPSIZE
MaxLines — setting a maximum number of lines for the text to display, useful for wrapping text
Marquee — enabling scrolling marquee effect on long text, often used in headers or titles
Justification — aligning text within the view using constants like LEFT or CENTER
Typeface — customizing font styles and families, including loading custom fonts from assets
TextAlignment — setting text alignment options like NATURAL or INHERIT

knowledge.what_it_is: A Frame in Android is a View that holds other Views, providing a way to structure and organize your app's UI components. It's a fundamental building block of any Android app.
what_you_will_learn: FrameLayout — a basic layout container that holds child views without any specific positioning or alignment
ConstraintLayout — a more advanced layout container that uses constraints to position and size its child views, providing a flexible and dynamic UI
LinearLayout — a horizontal or vertical layout container that arranges its child views in a linear fashion, useful for simple layouts
RelativeLayout — a layout container that positions its child views relative to each other, useful for complex layouts
FrameLayout vs ConstraintLayout — understanding the trade-offs between these two popular layout containers and when to use each one
Implementing a custom FrameLayout — learning how to extend the FrameLayout class to create a custom layout component with specific features or behavior

knowledge.what_it_is: A linear layout is a basic UI component in Android that arranges its children in a single row or column, allowing for flexible and dynamic layouts.
what_you_will_learn: LinearLayout — a versatile layout container that supports both horizontal and vertical orientations
Orientation — setting the orientation to either horizontal (LinearLayout.HORIZONTAL) or vertical (LinearLayout.VERTICAL)
Weighted Layouts — using weights to distribute space among children in a linear layout
Gravity — applying gravity to align children within their parent's bounds
Margin and Padding — understanding the difference between margin and padding, and how to use them to create spacing around children
Layout Parameters — learning about the various layout parameters that can be applied to children, such as width, height, and weight
Weight Sum — calculating the total weight of all children in a linear layout to ensure proper distribution of space

knowledge.what_it_is: App shortcuts are a feature in Android that allows users to quickly access frequently used app features or actions from the home screen, without launching the full app. They can be created using the ShortcutManager API and are displayed in the shortcut menu.
what_you_will_learn: ShortcutManager — creating and managing shortcuts programmatically
ShortcutInfo — representing a single shortcut with its icon, title, and intent
DynamicShortcuts — creating shortcuts that change based on user input or app state
StaticShortcuts — creating shortcuts that are always available and do not change
ShortcutIcon — specifying the icon to be used for a shortcut
IntentFilter — specifying the actions that can be performed by a shortcut
ShortcutCategory — categorizing shortcuts for better organization
PinnedShortcuts — pinning frequently used shortcuts to the home screen

knowledge.what_it_is: A button is a UI element that responds to user interaction, typically used for actions like clicking, tapping, or pressing. In Android, buttons are a crucial part of creating engaging and interactive interfaces.
what_you_will_learn: Button basics — understanding the different types of buttons (e.g., Button, ImageButton, ToggleButton) and their uses
XML layout attributes — learning how to customize button appearance using XML attributes like background, textColor, and textSize
OnClickListener — implementing a listener to handle button clicks and perform actions
Button states — understanding the different states a button can be in (e.g., enabled, disabled, pressed) and how to manage them
Material Design guidelines — applying Material Design principles for creating visually appealing and consistent buttons
Accessibility features — incorporating accessibility features like contentDescription and focusable to make buttons accessible to users with disabilities

knowledge.what_it_is: A bottom sheet is a type of floating panel that can be shown above or below other content in an Android app, providing additional information or actions without leaving the current screen. It's commonly used for settings, options, and navigation menus.
what_you_will_learn: BottomSheetDialogFragment — creating a basic bottom sheet using a DialogFragment
BottomSheetBehavior — implementing a bottom sheet with a scrollable content area and a floating action button
BottomSheetLayout — designing a custom bottom sheet layout with a header, footer, and content area
Material Components for Android (MDC-Android) — using the official library to implement a bottom sheet with Material Design guidelines
Customizing the peek height and elevation of a bottom sheet using setPeekHeight() and setElevation() methods
Handling bottom sheet state changes, such as opening and closing, using the onStateChanged() callback method

knowledge.what_it_is: A Dialog is a floating window that provides information to the user or prompts them to take an action, often used in Android applications to display alerts, warnings, and confirmations.
what_you_will_learn: AlertDialog — creating custom dialogs with buttons, checkboxes, and radio buttons
DialogFragment — using a Dialog as a Fragment for more flexibility and lifecycle management
Builder pattern — constructing dialog instances with a fluent API (e.g., AlertDialog.Builder)
Theme and style customization — applying custom themes and styles to dialogs
Accessibility features — making dialogs accessible to users with disabilities (e.g., TalkBack, screen readers)
Dialog dismissal and cancellation — handling user dismissals and cancellations of dialogs
AsyncTask or Coroutine usage — performing background tasks while displaying a dialog
Material Design guidelines — designing dialogs that follow Material Design principles

knowledge.what_it_is: A Relative Layout is a ViewGroup that allows you to position and size child views relative to each other or to the parent layout, without specifying an absolute position.
what_you_will_learn: Layout parameters — defining how children are laid out within the RelativeLayout
Margins and padding — understanding the difference between these two concepts for spacing between views
Weighted dimensions — using weights to define relative sizes of child views
Aligning views — using gravity and layout alignment attributes to position child views
Centering views — using the centerInParent attribute or Gravity.CENTER_HORIZONTAL/VERTICAL
Relative positioning with respect to other views — using the aboveLayout, belowLayout, leftOf, rightOf, alignBelow, alignAbove attributes
Using RelativeLayout with other layouts — combining Relative and other layouts like LinearLayout or ConstraintLayout

knowledge.what_it_is: Navigation Components are a part of Jetpack Navigation, which provides a consistent API surface for navigating between destinations in an app, replacing traditional FragmentManager and Activity APIs. It's designed to simplify navigation and improve code organization.
what_you_will_learn: NavController — the central hub for managing navigation, providing methods for navigating between destinations
NavGraph — a visual representation of the app's navigation structure, used to define the possible paths through the app
Destination — an abstract class representing a single point in the navigation graph, can be a Fragment or Activity
Action — a way to navigate from one destination to another, can be a simple action like navigating back or forward
Deep links — using Uri and Intent to navigate between apps or within an app, enabling features like sharing and deep linking
Safe Args — a library for generating safe, type-safe arguments for navigation actions, reducing the risk of null pointer exceptions
NavArgs — a way to pass data between destinations, including support for nullable types and default values

knowledge.what_it_is: A tabbed interface allows users to navigate between multiple screens within an app, improving user experience and reducing clutter.
what_you_will_learn: TabLayout — a widget that displays tabs at the top or bottom of the screen, used with ViewPager for smooth navigation
ViewPager — a widget that enables swipe gestures for navigating between tabs, often paired with TabLayout
FragmentStatePagerAdapter — an adapter for managing fragments within a ViewPager, suitable for most use cases
FragmentPagerAdapter — an older adapter for managing fragments within a ViewPager, still supported but less recommended
TabItem — a custom view that represents a single tab within the TabLayout, can be customized with icons and labels
setOnTabSelectedListener — a method for setting up listener callbacks when a tab is selected or deselected
getTabAt — a method for retrieving a specific TabItem at a given position within the TabLayout

knowledge.what_it_is: EditText is a user interface component in Android that allows users to input text, including single-line and multi-line text entry, password masking, and numeric input. It is a subclass of TextView and provides additional functionality for handling user input.
what_you_will_learn: XML attributes — configuring EditText properties such as hint, inputType, and maxLength in XML
EditText constructors — creating an instance of EditText with default or custom parameters, including context, layout params, and attributes
Input types — specifying the type of input allowed, such as text, phone, email, or password using android:inputType attribute
Hint and placeholder — displaying a hint or placeholder text to guide users when no text is entered
Password masking — hiding user input for passwords using android:inputType='textPassword'
Numeric input — restricting input to numbers only using android:inputType='numberDecimal' or android:inputType='numberSigned'
IME options — configuring the Input Method Editor (IME) behavior, such as allowing multiple lines of text entry
EditText states — understanding the different states an EditText can be in, including enabled, disabled, and focused
Accessibility features — making EditText accessible to users with disabilities using android:contentDescription attribute

knowledge.what_it_is: An ImageView is a widget that displays an image, used to display static or dynamic images in Android applications.
what_you_will_learn: XML attributes — setting properties like scaleType, cropToPadding, and adjustViewBounds
setImageResource() vs setImageDrawable() vs setImageBitmap() — choosing the right method for different use cases
setImageURI() — loading an image from a URI or file path
setScaleType() — scaling images to fit within the ImageView bounds
setAdjustViewBounds() — adjusting the ImageView bounds to match the image size
setCropToPadding() — cropping the image to match the padding of the ImageView
getLayoutParams() and setLayoutParams() — working with layout parameters for customizing the ImageView size
onDraw() and onLayout() — overriding these methods for custom drawing or layout logic

knowledge.what_it_is: Constraints are a layout mechanism introduced in Android Jetpack, allowing you to define relationships between views and their parents without using explicit layouts like LinearLayout or RelativeLayout.
what_you_will_learn: Layout constraints — defining relationships between views and their parents using the ConstraintLayout
Guidelines — horizontal and vertical lines that serve as anchors for view placement
Barrier — a virtual guide that can be used to create complex layout scenarios
Chain — a group of views connected by constraints, allowing for flexible layouts
Circular constraint — a type of constraint that connects two views in a circular relationship
Bias and margin — adjusting the position of a view relative to its parent or guideline
Inequality constraints — defining relationships between views based on their sizes or positions

knowledge.what_it_is: A ListView is a widget in Android that displays a list of items, typically with a scrollable layout and customizable appearance.
what_you_will_learn: Adapter — an abstract class that provides data to the ListView, implementing getView(), getItemId(), getCount() methods
ListView.LayoutParams — setting dimensions, margins, padding for individual list items
ListFragment — a built-in Android fragment for displaying lists with minimal setup
CursorLoader — loading and managing cursor-based data sources for ListView
ViewHolder — optimizing performance by reusing views in the ListView adapter
ItemClickListeners — handling user interactions with list items, such as clicks or long presses

knowledge.what_it_is: Android animations are used to create visually appealing and engaging user experiences by adding motion to UI elements, such as views, layouts, and transitions between screens.
what_you_will_learn: ObjectAnimator — a powerful animation class for animating properties of views, including position, size, rotation, and more
ValueAnimator — a basic animation class for animating values over time, often used in conjunction with ObjectAnimator
AnimatorSet — a container class that allows you to combine multiple animations into a single set, enabling complex animation sequences
PropertyValuesHolder — a class that defines a set of properties and their corresponding animation values, used within Animator objects
EasingInterpolator — an interface for defining custom easing functions to control the speed and acceleration of animations
AnimatorListener — an interface for listening to animation events, such as start, end, and cancel
AnimationSet — a class that allows you to combine multiple animations into a single set, enabling complex animation sequences
LayoutTransition — a class that provides automatic layout transitions between views, including fade-in, slide-in, and more

knowledge.what_it_is: A Drawer, also known as Navigation Drawer or Sliding Menu, is a UI component that provides a way to navigate through different screens and features of an Android app. It is typically placed on the left side of the screen and can be toggled open by swiping from the edge.
what_you_will_learn: DrawerLayout — a container view group for hosting drawers, providing layout management and animation
NavigationView — a widget that provides a pre-designed navigation drawer with a list of menu items and icons
MenuInflater — an API for inflating menu resources into the Drawer's menu
MenuItem — a class representing a single item in the Drawer's menu, with properties like title, icon, and action
DrawerListener — an interface for listening to events related to the Drawer's state (open, close, drag)
GestureDetectorCompat — a utility class for detecting gestures on the screen, including swipe actions
AppBarConfiguration — a data structure for configuring the behavior of the AppBar in conjunction with the Drawer

knowledge.what_it_is: RecyclerView is a flexible and efficient widget for displaying large datasets in Android, introduced in API level 21 (Lollipop). It provides features like layout management, animation, and scrolling. RecyclerView is designed to work with Adapter classes that bind data to views.
what_you_will_learn: Adapter — a bridge between the data source and the RecyclerView, responsible for binding data to views
ViewHolder — a container for holding the views of a single item in the list, used to improve performance by reducing view inflation
LayoutManager — responsible for arranging items within the RecyclerView, including linear, grid, and staggered layouts
ItemAnimator — controls the animation of items as they are inserted or removed from the RecyclerView
SpanSizeLookup — determines how many spans a particular item should occupy in a multi-span layout
DiffUtil — a utility class for calculating the differences between two lists and updating the RecyclerView accordingly
RecyclerView.ViewHolder.getAdapterPosition() — returns the position of the view holder within its adapter
RecyclerView.Adapter.notifyDataSetChanged() — notifies the RecyclerView that its underlying data has changed

knowledge.what_it_is: Model-View-Intent (MVI) is an architectural pattern for Android applications, designed to handle complex business logic and provide a clear separation of concerns between data, presentation, and user interactions. It was popularized by Jake Wharton's article 'MVI on Android' in 2017.
what_you_will_learn: Intent — the central concept in MVI, representing a single action or request from the user
Model — the data layer, responsible for managing business logic and state
View — the presentation layer, responsible for rendering the UI and handling user interactions
ViewState — the current state of the View, used to determine what actions are possible
Action — a specific intent that can be triggered by the user or other components
EffectHandler — handles side effects such as network requests, database operations, etc.
Store — a centralized repository for storing and retrieving application state
Reducer — a pure function that takes in the current state and an action to produce a new state
ViewStateTransformer — transforms ViewState into a format suitable for rendering

knowledge.what_it_is: Model-View-ViewModel (MVVM) is an architectural pattern for Android app development, designed to separate business logic from UI components and make code more maintainable. It uses data-binding and observable properties to update views automatically.
what_you_will_learn: Data Binding — declarative syntax for binding UI elements to data models using layouts and XML attributes
ViewModel — a singleton class that holds and manages business logic, exposing data through observables
LiveData — a lifecycle-aware observable data holder that notifies views of changes in real-time
Repository Pattern — encapsulating data access and business logic within a single layer for easier maintenance
MVC vs MVVM — understanding the differences between Model-View-Controller (MVC) and Model-View-ViewModel (MVVM)
AndroidX Lifecycle — integrating lifecycle-aware components with ViewModel and LiveData for robust state management
Dependency Injection — using Dagger or Hilt to decouple dependencies and improve testability
RxJava/RxKotlin — reactive programming libraries for handling asynchronous data streams and events
Room Persistence Library — a SQLite wrapper that simplifies database operations and provides caching
Coroutines — Kotlin's concurrency library for writing efficient, asynchronous code using suspend functions

knowledge.what_it_is: Minimum Viable Product (MVP) is an Android development approach that involves creating a product with just enough features to satisfy early customers and provide feedback for future development. It's a lean startup methodology that helps developers prioritize and focus on essential features.
what_you_will_learn: Defining MVP goals and objectives — setting clear targets for the initial release
Prioritizing features using MoSCoW method or Kano model — identifying must-haves, should-haves, could-haves, and won't-haves
Designing a minimal viable architecture — selecting the right tech stack and frameworks for MVP
Implementing incremental development — breaking down large tasks into smaller, manageable chunks
Testing and validating MVP features — using unit tests, integration tests, and UI automation to ensure quality
Gathering user feedback and iterating on MVP — collecting data from early adopters and making informed decisions for future development

knowledge.what_it_is: Model-View-Controller (MVC) is an architectural pattern used in Android app development to separate application logic into three interconnected components: Model, View, and Controller. It helps maintain a clean and organized codebase by promoting loose coupling and reusability.
what_you_will_learn: Models — data storage and management using Room Persistence Library or SQLiteOpenHelper
Views — user interface components such as Activity, Fragment, and ViewGroup, created with XML layouts or programmatically
Controllers — handling user interactions and business logic using Android's built-in APIs (e.g., Intent, BroadcastReceiver)
Activity lifecycle — understanding the different states an Activity can be in (created, started, resumed, paused, stopped, destroyed) and how to manage them
Fragment management — creating, managing, and communicating between Fragments using FragmentManager and FragmentTransaction
Data binding — using DataBindingUtil or ViewBinding to bind data to Views and simplify UI updates
Dependency injection — using Dagger or Hilt to decouple dependencies and make the app more modular and testable

knowledge.what_it_is: The Repository pattern is a design pattern that abstracts data access and business logic from the presentation layer, making it easier to manage complex data operations and decouple dependencies. In Android development, it's commonly used with Room persistence library or other local storage solutions.
what_you_will_learn: Repository interface — defining a contract for data retrieval and manipulation
Local data source implementation (e.g., Room database) — encapsulating data access logic
Remote data source implementation (e.g., Retrofit, OkHttp) — handling network requests and caching
Data mapping and transformation — converting between data models and presentation layer requirements
Caching strategies — implementing cache expiration policies and eviction mechanisms
Repository implementation — combining local and remote data sources to provide a unified API
Dependency injection — using Dagger, Hilt, or other libraries to manage repository instances

knowledge.what_it_is: The Builder pattern is a design pattern that separates the construction of complex objects from their representation, allowing the same construction process to create different representations. It's commonly used in Android development to simplify the creation of complex objects such as UI components or data models.
what_you_will_learn: IntentBuilder — creating Intent objects with a fluent API for specifying actions and extras
ParcelableCreator — implementing the Parcelable interface using the Builder pattern for efficient serialization
ViewModelFactory — using the Builder pattern to create ViewModel instances with dependency injection
RepositoryPatternBuilder — applying the Builder pattern to simplify data repository construction
DataModelBuilder — creating complex data models using a fluent API and the Builder pattern
RecyclerViewAdapterBuilder — building RecyclerView adapters with a Builder pattern for efficient item creation

knowledge.what_it_is: The Factory Pattern is a creational design pattern that provides an interface for creating objects without specifying the exact class of object to be created, allowing for more flexibility and extensibility in software development. In Android, it's commonly used to create instances of View classes or other UI components.
what_you_will_learn: Factory methods — defining a method that returns an instance of a specific class without specifying the exact class name
Abstract Factory Pattern — providing a way to encapsulate a group of individual factories that have a common theme, allowing for more flexibility and extensibility in software development
Builder Pattern vs. Factory Pattern — understanding when to use each pattern and how they differ in Android app development
Dependency Injection with Factory Pattern — using the Factory Pattern to provide dependencies to objects without hardcoding them
Kotlin's Factory Functions — using Kotlin's built-in support for factory functions to create instances of classes
AndroidX's Factory Classes — using AndroidX's factory classes, such as LayoutInflater and ViewFactory, to create views and other UI components
subtopics: builder pattern vs factory pattern android
dependency injection with factory pattern android

knowledge.what_it_is: The Observer pattern is a design pattern that allows an object to notify other objects about changes to its state, decoupling the subject from its observers. In Android, it's commonly used in data binding and UI updates.
what_you_will_learn: Observer interface — defining the contract for observer objects to receive notifications
Subject class — managing a collection of observers and notifying them about changes
Registering and unregistering observers — adding or removing observers from the subject's list
Notify observers method — sending updates to all registered observers when the subject's state changes
RxJava/RxKotlin Observables — using reactive extensions for asynchronous data processing and observer management
LiveData class — a lifecycle-aware observable that notifies observers about data changes, ideal for Android UI components

knowledge.what_it_is: LiveData is an observable data holder class in Android that allows data to be exposed as a stream of updates, while keeping the data up-to-date and handling configuration changes.
what_you_will_learn: Lifecycle-awareness — LiveData's ability to automatically notify observers when the underlying data changes or the lifecycle of the observer changes
Value and MutableLiveData — understanding the difference between a regular LiveData object and its mutable counterpart, which allows for direct modification of the data
Observer pattern — how LiveData uses the Observer pattern to notify registered observers about changes in the data
Reactive programming — LiveData's support for reactive programming principles, allowing developers to write more efficient and scalable code
Configuration change handling — how LiveData automatically handles configuration changes, such as screen rotations or device settings changes
Caching and persistence — understanding how LiveData can be used to cache and persist data across app restarts and configuration changes

knowledge.what_it_is: Dependency injection (DI) is a software design pattern that allows components to be loosely coupled, making it easier to test, maintain and extend the codebase. In Android, DI is used to manage dependencies between objects, reducing the need for hard-coded references.
what_you_will_learn: Service Locator — a central registry of singleton instances that can be injected into other components
Dagger 2 — a popular dependency injection library for Android, providing compile-time and runtime checks
Hilt — a modern, annotation-based dependency injection library developed by Google, replacing Dagger 2 in many projects
Singletons vs. Interfaces — understanding the trade-offs between using singletons or interfaces to manage dependencies
Contextual Dependencies — injecting context-dependent dependencies, such as Activity or Fragment instances
Lazy Loading — loading dependencies only when needed, improving app performance and reducing memory usage

knowledge.what_it_is: Shared Preferences is a built-in Android API that allows storing and retrieving key-value pairs, used to persist data between app restarts or sessions.
what_you_will_learn: SharedPreferences — accessing and manipulating shared preferences using SharedPreferences.Editor
Context.getSharedPreferences() — obtaining a SharedPreferences instance from an Activity or Application context
MODE_PRIVATE — specifying the default access mode for shared preferences (only accessible by this app)
MODE_WORLD_READABLE/MODE_WORLD_WRITEABLE — deprecated modes, not recommended for new apps
getSharedPreferences() vs getPreferences() — understanding the difference between these two methods
putString(), putInt(), putBoolean(), etc. — storing primitive data types using SharedPreferences.Editor
getString(), getInt(), getBoolean(), etc. — retrieving stored values from SharedPreferences
clear() and edit() — clearing shared preferences and modifying existing entries

knowledge.what_it_is: A DataStore is a persistent storage solution that allows Android apps to store and manage data locally, providing a simple and efficient way to persist data between app sessions. It was introduced as part of Jetpack Compose and is designed to replace traditional SQLite databases.
what_you_will_learn: Room persistence library — the official Android persistence library for storing data in a local database
DataStore API — the interface used to interact with DataStores, including methods for inserting, updating, and deleting data
Flow and Coroutines — how to use Kotlin's Flow and Coroutines APIs to handle asynchronous operations when working with DataStores
Type-safe queries — how to write type-safe queries using the DataStore API, eliminating the need for raw SQL
DataStore types — understanding the different types of DataStores available, including Room and other third-party options
DataStore lifecycle management — how to manage the lifecycle of a DataStore, including handling configuration changes and app restarts

knowledge.what_it_is: Room Persistence Library is an SQLite-based persistence library that provides a simple, typesafe database access layer for Android apps. It was introduced by Google as a replacement for SQLiteOpenHelper and has since become the recommended way to manage databases in Android.
what_you_will_learn: Entities — defining data models using Java annotations (e.g., @Entity, @PrimaryKey)
DAOs — creating data access objects that interact with the database (e.g., RoomDatabase, DAO interface)
Queries — writing SQL queries to retrieve or modify data (e.g., SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE)
RoomDatabase — extending this class to create a custom database instance
LiveData — using Room's built-in LiveData support for observing changes in the database
TypeConverters — defining custom type converters for mapping between Java types and SQLite types
Callbacks — handling asynchronous operations (e.g., insert, update, delete) with callbacks
RxJava Support — using Room's RxJava wrapper to interact with the database asynchronously

knowledge.what_it_is: The Android file system is a hierarchical structure that stores and organizes data on an Android device, including apps, settings, and user-generated content. It is based on the Linux file system and provides access to files through various APIs.
what_you_will_learn: File paths — understanding how to construct and navigate file paths in Android using Uri, File, and Path classes
Internal vs external storage — managing data storage on internal device storage (e.g., /data) and external storage (e.g., SD cards)
Context.getCacheDir() and Context.getExternalFilesDir() — accessing cache and external files directories for app-specific storage needs
FileIO and FileOutputStream — reading and writing files using Android's built-in file input/output APIs
SharedPreferences vs FileStorage — choosing between SharedPreferences and file-based storage for app settings and data
Android's ContentProvider API — securely sharing files and data between apps through a provider-client architecture
MediaStore and MediaScannerConnection — accessing and scanning media files (e.g., images, videos) on the device
File encryption using Android KeyStore — securing sensitive data with hardware-backed key storage

knowledge.what_it_is: Networking is a crucial aspect of Android app development, enabling communication between devices and servers over various protocols. It involves understanding how to handle network requests, manage connections, and optimize data transfer.
what_you_will_learn: TCP/IP model — the fundamental protocol suite for internet communication, including transport (TCP/UDP), internet (IP), and application layers
HTTP/2 — a modern, binary-based protocol for multiplexing multiple requests over a single connection, improving performance and reducing overhead
OkHttp — a popular Android library for making HTTP requests, providing features like caching, connection pooling, and SSL/TLS support
Retrofit — a type-safe HTTP client for Android, allowing developers to define API interfaces using annotations and automatically generating boilerplate code
Socket programming — creating network sockets for bidirectional communication between devices, including TCP and UDP socket types
Network protocols — understanding the differences between HTTP, HTTPS, FTP, SFTP, SSH, and other protocols used in Android app development
Android Network Security Configuration — a feature for configuring network security policies, such as SSL/TLS pinning and certificate verification
Volley — an older but still widely-used networking library for Android, providing features like caching and connection pooling
URLConnection — a built-in Android class for making HTTP requests, offering basic functionality for downloading files and sending data over the network
AsyncTask — a deprecated but still useful utility class for performing background tasks, including network operations and database queries

knowledge.what_it_is: Retrofit is an Android library developed by Square (now part of Stripe) that simplifies network calls from Android applications, providing a declarative way to create and manage HTTP requests. It's widely used for its simplicity, flexibility, and ability to handle complex networking scenarios.
what_you_will_learn: OkHttp client — using Retrofit with OkHttp as the underlying HTTP client
Interface-based API design — defining APIs using Java interfaces annotated with Retrofit annotations
Method annotations — using @GET, @POST, @PUT, @DELETE to specify request methods
Parameter annotations — using @Query, @Path, @Body to specify query parameters and request bodies
Converter annotations — using @SerializedName, @Expose to handle JSON serialization and deserialization
Response handling — using Retrofit's built-in response handlers or custom ones for error handling
Async calls — making asynchronous network calls using Retrofit's coroutine support
RxJava integration — combining Retrofit with RxJava for reactive programming

knowledge.what_it_is: OkHttp is an open-source, asynchronous HTTP client library for Android applications, providing efficient and customizable networking capabilities.
what_you_will_learn: OkHttp Interceptors — using interceptors to modify or extend request/response behavior, such as adding headers or logging
Request/Response Builders — creating requests and responses programmatically using OkHttp's builder API
Async HTTP Requests — making asynchronous HTTP requests using OkHttp's synchronous and asynchronous APIs
Connection Pooling — reusing connections to improve performance and reduce latency
HTTP Method Support — sending GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, HEAD, OPTIONS, and PATCH requests
Header Management — setting and getting request/response headers, including custom headers
Cookie Management — handling cookies in HTTP requests and responses
Redirect Handling — following redirects automatically or manually using OkHttp's API

knowledge.what_it_is: Apollo Android is a GraphQL client library for Android, providing an easy-to-use API for interacting with GraphQL APIs. It's designed to work seamlessly with Retrofit and OkHttp, making it a popular choice for Android developers.
what_you_will_learn: GraphQL basics — understanding queries, mutations, and subscriptions
Apollo Client setup — configuring the client instance, setting up network interceptors
Querying data — using Apollo's query builder to fetch data from GraphQL APIs
Caching data — leveraging Apollo's built-in caching mechanism for offline support
Handling errors — catching and displaying error messages in your app
Apollo Link — understanding how to use the link API to customize network behavior
Type-safe queries — using Kotlin's type safety features with Apollo's query builder

knowledge.what_it_is: Coroutines are a design pattern that allows developers to write asynchronous code using a synchronous syntax, making it easier to manage complex tasks and operations on Android devices.
what_you_will_learn: Kotlin Coroutines — building blocks for writing asynchronous code in Kotlin
CoroutineScope — managing the lifecycle of coroutines and their context
Job and CancelToken — canceling and managing coroutine jobs
Async/Await — using suspend functions to write asynchronous code with a synchronous syntax
Suspend Functions — defining functions that can be suspended and resumed at specific points
Coroutine Context — passing data between coroutines using the context
Channel — communicating between coroutines using channels
Flow — building streams of data that can be processed asynchronously
Coroutine Dispatcher — scheduling coroutines on different threads or dispatchers
CoroutineExceptionHandler — handling exceptions thrown by coroutines
CoroutineScope's coroutineContext — accessing and modifying the coroutine context
Kotlinx.coroutines library — a set of extensions for working with coroutines in Kotlin

knowledge.what_it_is: In Android, threads are used to perform tasks that don't block the main thread, such as network requests, database operations, and computations. They run concurrently with the main thread, improving app responsiveness.
what_you_will_learn: Thread creation — creating a new Thread object using the Thread class or an ExecutorService
Thread states — understanding the different states of a thread (NEW, RUNNABLE, BLOCKED, WAITING, TIMED_WAITING, TERMINATED)
Thread synchronization — using locks (synchronized methods/blocking), semaphores, and monitors to protect shared resources
Thread communication — using wait(), notify(), and notifyAll() for inter-thread communication
Handler threads — using Handler objects to communicate with the main thread from a background thread
AsyncTask — understanding how AsyncTask works under the hood and its usage in Android
ExecutorService — creating and managing a pool of threads using ExecutorService
ThreadLocal variables — storing thread-local data using ThreadLocal variables

knowledge.what_it_is: RxJava is a Java library for composing asynchronous and event-driven programs using observable sequences, designed to simplify concurrency management and reactive programming on Android.
what_you_will_learn: Observables — creating and managing streams of data with operators like map, filter, reduce
Operators — chaining methods to transform and manipulate observables (e.g., flatMap, switchIfEmpty)
Subscriptions — handling observable lifecycles, including subscription management and cancellation
Schedulers — executing tasks on different threads or schedulers (e.g., Schedulers.io(), Schedulers.computation())
Subjects — sharing data between Observers using PublishSubject, BehaviorSubject, or ReplaySubject
RxJava Operators vs. Java 8 Streams — understanding the differences and when to use each
Error Handling — managing errors with operators like onErrorResumeNext, retryWhen, and catch

knowledge.what_it_is: RxKotlin is a Kotlin extension of ReactiveX, providing a declarative way to handle asynchronous data streams and events in Android applications.
what_you_will_learn: Operators — functions that transform or combine Observables (map, filter, flatMap, merge, zip)
Schedulers — managing concurrency for Observables (Schedulers.io, Schedulers.newThread, Schedulers.computation)
Subscription and Disposable — handling lifecycle of Observers and preventing memory leaks
Error Handling — propagating and catching errors in Observables (onErrorResumeNext, onErrorReturnItem)
Cold vs Hot Observables — understanding the difference between cold and hot Observables and when to use each
Subject — a special type of Observable that allows for bidirectional communication with Observers
RxJava interoperability — using RxKotlin with RxJava in Android applications

knowledge.what_it_is: Flow is an Android Jetpack library that simplifies data management and makes it easier to handle complex business logic, introduced by Google in 2017.
what_you_will_learn: What is Flow — a high-level abstraction for managing data and business logic, replacing traditional models and repositories
Data classes — defining immutable data structures with Kotlin's data class syntax, used as the foundation of Flow
Flow operators — building complex data pipelines using functional programming concepts like map, filter, and reduce
Transformations — applying operations to data flows, such as mapping, filtering, and combining data from multiple sources
Collectors — specifying how to handle the final result of a flow, including caching, storing in Room, or emitting events
Flow lifecycle management — understanding when to start and stop flows, and handling errors and exceptions
Error handling — using try-catch blocks and Flow's built-in error handling mechanisms to catch and recover from errors

knowledge.what_it_is: WorkManager is an Android Jetpack library that helps manage and execute background tasks, ensuring they run at the right time, even if the app is terminated or the device restarts. It provides a flexible and efficient way to schedule work, making it easier to handle complex task execution scenarios.
what_you_will_learn: WorkRequest — a request for executing a specific task, which can be periodic, one-time, or delayed
WorkManager Instance — creating an instance of WorkManager to manage tasks and schedules
Constraints — specifying constraints such as network type, battery level, or device idle state to execute tasks
Schedulers — using built-in schedulers like WORK_CONTINUOUSLY or WORK.Enqueue unique task instances
Periodic Tasks — scheduling periodic tasks with a specific interval and flex time
One-Time Tasks — executing one-time tasks at a specified time or delay
WorkContinuation — chaining multiple tasks together to create complex workflows
WorkListener — listening for work completion, cancellation, or failure events

knowledge.what_it_is: Authentication is the process of verifying user identity and ensuring secure access to Android applications, using various techniques such as username/password, biometrics (fingerprint, face recognition), or third-party services like Google Sign-In.
what_you_will_learn: OAuth2 — an authorization framework for secure client-server interactions, commonly used in Android apps for authentication with external services
Google Sign-In API — a service that allows users to sign-in to your app using their Google account credentials, providing a seamless and secure experience

Firebase Authentication — a cloud-based service that provides authentication as a service, allowing developers to easily integrate authentication into their Android apps without managing the underlying infrastructure
Biometric Authentication (Fingerprint, Face Recognition) — using device hardware to authenticate users, improving security and user experience
Password-Based Authentication — storing and verifying user passwords securely, handling password hashing, salting, and storage in Android apps
Session Management — understanding how to manage user sessions securely, including session expiration, revocation, and renewal
Token-Based Authentication (JWT) — using JSON Web Tokens to authenticate users, providing a secure and lightweight solution for authentication
Android KeyStore — a secure storage system that allows developers to store sensitive data like encryption keys and certificates securely on the device

knowledge.what_it_is: Crashlytics is a popular crash reporting and analytics tool developed by Firebase (now Google Cloud), used to monitor, report, and fix crashes in Android apps. It provides detailed stack traces, user data, and custom event tracking.
what_you_will_learn: Crashlytics SDK integration — adding Crashlytics library to your app's build.gradle file and initializing the SDK
Exception handling — catching and reporting exceptions with Crashlytics using try-catch blocks and Crashlytics.logException() method
Crash reporting — understanding crash reports, including stack traces, user data, and custom event tracking
Custom events — sending custom events to Crashlytics for analytics and logging purposes
User data collection — understanding how Crashlytics collects user data, such as device information and app usage metrics
Crash grouping — identifying and reporting similar crashes using Crashlytics' crash grouping feature
NDK integration — integrating Crashlytics with native libraries (NDK) for reporting crashes in native code

knowledge.what_it_is: Remote Config is a service provided by Firebase that allows you to store and manage configuration data for your Android app, which can be updated remotely without requiring an app update. It provides a simple way to manage feature flags, A/B testing, and other configurations.
what_you_will_learn: Firebase Remote Config — setting up the service in your Firebase console and integrating it with your Android app
Configuration data storage — storing key-value pairs, arrays, and nested objects in Remote Config
Feature flagging — using Remote Config to toggle features on or off for specific users or groups
A/B testing — using Remote Config to run experiments and measure the impact of different configurations on user behavior
Conditional logic — using Remote Config to apply conditions to feature flags and A/B tests based on user properties, device information, or other factors
Data validation — using Remote Config's built-in validation rules to ensure that configuration data is valid and consistent
Cache management — understanding how Remote Config caches configuration data locally on the device and how to manage cache expiration and refresh
Error handling — understanding how to handle errors when fetching or applying configuration data from Remote Config
subtopics: feature flagging with firebase remote config
ab testing with firebase remote config

knowledge.what_it_is: Google Cloud Messaging (GCM) and Firebase Cloud Messaging (FCM) are cloud-based services that enable Android apps to send targeted, personalized messages to users. FCM is a more recent replacement for GCM, offering improved performance, reliability, and scalability.
what_you_will_learn: Firebase Cloud Messaging (FCM) — the successor to Google Cloud Messaging (GCM), with improved performance and features
FCM Tokens — unique identifiers assigned to each device, used for sending targeted messages
Message Types — understanding the different types of messages supported by FCM, including notification and data messages
Message Priority — setting message priority levels to ensure timely delivery, including high, normal, and low priorities
Message Delivery Options — configuring options for handling message delivery, such as retries and exponential backoff
FCM Registration Tokens — obtaining and managing registration tokens for devices, including token refresh and revocation
FCM Messaging Formats — understanding the different messaging formats supported by FCM, including JSON and XML
FCM Webhooks — setting up webhooks to receive notifications when messages are delivered or failed

knowledge.what_it_is: Firebase Firestore is a NoSQL document database that allows Android developers to store and sync data across clients, servers, and cloud functions. It provides real-time data synchronization, offline support, and scalable storage.
what_you_will_learn: Firestore Database — structuring data into collections, documents, and fields
Cloud Firestore SDK for Android — integrating Firestore with Android apps using the official SDK
Data Modeling — designing data structures to optimize querying and indexing in Firestore
Realtime Data Synchronization — understanding how Firestore maintains real-time consistency across clients and servers
Offline Support — enabling offline access to Firestore data and syncing changes when online again
Security Rules — defining access control rules for Firestore collections and documents using Cloud Firestore Security Rules
Data Retrieval — querying Firestore data using the Query API, including filtering, sorting, and limiting results
Document Snapshots — working with document snapshots to handle concurrent updates and conflicts

knowledge.what_it_is: Google AdMob is a mobile advertising platform that allows developers to monetize their Android apps through banner, interstitial, rewarded video, and native ads. It provides a range of tools and features to help developers optimize ad performance, including mediation, targeting, and analytics.
what_you_will_learn: Ad formats — understanding the different types of ads supported by AdMob, such as banner, interstitial, and rewarded video
Ad unit IDs — creating and managing unique identifiers for each ad placement in your app
Mediation — using AdMob's mediation feature to connect multiple ad networks and optimize ad revenue
Targeting — setting up targeting options to reach specific audiences based on demographics, interests, or behaviors
Ad requests — making ad requests to AdMob's servers and handling responses, including error handling and caching
Ad events — tracking and reporting ad-related events, such as clicks, impressions, and completions
Rewarded video ads — implementing rewarded video ads in your app, including setting up rewards and tracking user engagement
subtopics: ad requests (ad request object, ad response object)
rewarded video ads (setting up rewards, tracking user engagement)
