knowledge.what_it_is: Google Play Services (GPS) is a set of APIs and services provided by Google to help Android developers implement common features such as authentication, location, and analytics. It is a crucial component of the Android ecosystem and is used by millions of apps worldwide.
what_you_will_learn: Google Sign-In — authenticating users with their Google account credentials
Google Maps SDK — integrating Google Maps into your app for navigation and location-based services
Firebase Authentication — using Firebase's authentication system to manage user accounts and sessions
Play Games Services — implementing leaderboards, achievements, and multiplayer features in your game
Google Cloud Messaging (GCM) — sending push notifications to users with GCM's cloud-based messaging service
In-App Purchasing (IAP) — handling transactions and subscriptions using Google Play Billing
Location Services — accessing device location data with GPS, Wi-Fi, and cellular network information
Google Analytics — tracking app usage, user behavior, and other metrics with Google's analytics platform

knowledge.what_it_is: Google Maps is a popular mapping and navigation service provided by Google, which can be integrated into Android applications to provide location-based features such as maps, routes, and places.
what_you_will_learn: Getting an API key for Google Maps — obtaining a unique identifier to use the Google Maps Android API
Using the Google Maps Android API Client Library — importing and using the official library for interacting with the Google Maps service
Displaying maps on an Android device — adding a MapView or MapFragment to your app to display maps
Handling map gestures and events — responding to user interactions such as taps, long presses, and drags
Adding markers and overlays — placing custom icons or shapes on the map to highlight specific locations
Calculating routes and distances — using the Google Maps API to determine the shortest path between two points
Geocoding and reverse geocoding — converting addresses to latitude/longitude coordinates and vice versa
subtopics: google maps android api client library
validation_keywords: google maps android api client library

knowledge.what_it_is: Dagger is an open-source, dependency injection library developed by Google, designed to simplify Android app architecture and reduce boilerplate code. It's a popular choice among Android developers due to its ease of use and robust features.
what_you_will_learn: Modules — defining components that provide dependencies using the @Module annotation
Components — creating instances of modules and their dependencies using the @Component annotation
Injecting dependencies — using the @Inject annotation to inject dependencies into classes
Scopes — controlling the lifecycle of objects injected by Dagger (e.g., @Singleton, @ActivityScoped)
Providers — creating instances of components and injecting them into other components
Subcomponents — defining sub-components that depend on parent components' dependencies
Multibinding — providing multiple instances of a component or module
Factory methods — using the @Binds annotation to provide factory methods for modules
Custom scopes — creating custom scope annotations to control object lifecycles

knowledge.what_it_is: Koin is an open-source, lightweight dependency injection framework for Android applications, designed to simplify and modularize app architecture.
what_you_will_learn: Modules — defining logical groups of components and services using @Module annotation
Components — injecting dependencies into Android classes (Activities, Fragments, Services) using @Component annotation
Singletons — creating singleton instances of components or services for global access
Lazy loading — delaying the creation of components or services until they are actually needed
Scopes — defining the lifecycle of a component or service instance (e.g., per activity, per fragment)
ViewModel injection — injecting ViewModels into Android classes using @ViewModelStoreOwner annotation
Koin Logger — logging and debugging Koin-related events and errors

knowledge.what_it_is: Hilt is a dependency injection library developed by Google, designed to simplify and standardize dependency injection in Android applications.
what_you_will_learn: Dependency Injection — injecting dependencies into components instead of creating them manually
Modules — defining modules that provide dependencies for other components
Components — using the @AndroidEntryPoint annotation to enable Hilt support for Activities, Fragments, and Services
Providers — using the @Provides annotation to define providers for dependencies
Singletons — managing singleton instances of classes using the @Singleton annotation
Scoped Instances — creating scoped instances of classes using the @Named annotation
Injecting Dependencies — injecting dependencies into components using the @Inject annotation
Hilt Modules — defining Hilt modules that provide dependencies for other components
AndroidEntryPoint — enabling Hilt support for Activities, Fragments, and Services

knowledge.what_it_is: Kodein is an Android dependency injection framework that simplifies the process of managing dependencies between components, making it easier to write modular and testable code. It provides a simple and intuitive API for injecting dependencies into objects.
what_you_will_learn: Dependency Injection — a design pattern for decoupling components from their dependencies
Kodein Module — a container that holds dependencies and makes them available to components
Singleton Scope — a scope where instances are shared across the application
Eager Singleton Scope — a scope where instances are created eagerly when the module is initialized
Lazy Singleton Scope — a scope where instances are created lazily when requested by a component
Named Scope — a scope where instances can be retrieved by name, useful for sharing resources between components
Provider — a class that provides dependencies to components, can be used to create custom providers
Module Builder — a builder pattern for creating modules with specific configurations and scopes

knowledge.what_it_is: Espresso is an Android testing framework developed by Google, designed to help developers write and run unit tests for their app's UI components. It provides a simple and efficient way to test individual UI elements, such as buttons, text fields, and layouts.
what_you_will_learn: Assertions — using matchers like onView(), onData() or onChildView() to verify UI element properties
Actions — performing actions like click(), typeText() or swipe() on UI elements
UI components — testing individual UI components, such as buttons, text fields and layouts
Layouts — verifying layout-related properties, such as size, position and orientation
Views — testing views, including their visibility, content description and accessibility features
Intents — testing app's intent handling, including launching activities and services
Rules — using rules like atLeast() or atMost() to configure test execution and timing
Test lifecycle — understanding Espresso's test lifecycle, including setup, teardown and test execution

knowledge.what_it_is: JUnit is an open-source unit testing framework for Java, widely used in Android app development to ensure code reliability and maintainability.
what_you_will_learn: Test classes — writing JUnit test cases as separate classes from the main application code
Assertions — using assertEquals(), assertTrue(), assertFalse() to verify expected results
Mocking objects — using Mockito to isolate dependencies and test individual components in isolation
Test suites — grouping related tests together for easier execution and maintenance
Junit4 vs JUnit5 — understanding the differences between these two versions, including new features like parameterized testing
Parameterized testing — using @RunWith(Parameterized.class) to run the same test multiple times with different inputs
Test lifecycle methods — understanding the role of setup(), tearDown(), and after() in managing test execution flow

knowledge.what_it_is: Timber is a popular logging library for Android, designed to simplify and standardize logging across an application.
what_you_will_learn: Log levels — understanding the different log levels (DEBUG, INFO, WARN, ERROR, VERBOSE) and when to use each
Logging with Timber — how to use Timber to log messages at various levels, including custom log tags and formatting
TimberTree — creating a TimberTree instance to manage logging across an application, including setting the log level and output
Customizing log output — using Timber's API to customize the format of logged messages, including adding metadata and filtering logs
Async logging — understanding how Timber handles asynchronous logging and when to use it for performance-critical code
Integration with other libraries — integrating Timber with other popular Android libraries, such as Crashlytics or Firebase Analytics

knowledge.what_it_is: Leak Canary is an open-source library for detecting memory leaks in Android applications, developed by Square (now part of GitHub). It provides a simple and effective way to identify memory leaks and optimize app performance.
what_you_will_learn: What are memory leaks — understanding the concept and its impact on app performance
LeakCanary architecture — how it works under the hood, including the Reference Tracker and Memory Profiler
Memory leak detection — how to use LeakCanary to identify memory leaks in your app, including the use of the Analysis Activity
Heap analysis — understanding how to analyze heap dumps using tools like Android Studio's Memory Monitor or MAT
Garbage collection — how Java's garbage collector works and how it relates to memory leaks
Object retention — understanding how objects are retained in memory and how to identify retainers using LeakCanary
LeakCanary integration — how to integrate LeakCanary into your app, including the use of the Application class
Best practices for memory management — understanding how to write efficient code that minimizes memory leaks

knowledge.what_it_is: Chucker is an open-source, real-time memory monitoring tool for Android apps, designed to help developers identify and fix memory-related issues. It provides detailed information about memory usage, including heap size, allocation rates, and object lifetimes.
what_you_will_learn: Memory Monitoring — understanding how Chucker monitors memory usage in real-time, including heap size, allocation rates, and object lifetimes
Heap Dump Analysis — learning to analyze heap dumps generated by Chucker to identify memory leaks and optimize app performance
Object Lifetimes — understanding how Chucker tracks the creation and destruction of objects in the app's heap
Memory Leaks Detection — identifying common causes of memory leaks, such as retained references and circular object graphs
Chucker UI Components — learning to use Chucker's UI components to visualize memory usage data and identify performance bottlenecks
Customizing Chucker — understanding how to customize Chucker's behavior and appearance to suit specific development needs

knowledge.what_it_is: Jetpack Benchmark is an Android library that provides a simple and efficient way to measure the performance of your app's UI components, helping you identify bottlenecks and optimize your code. It's part of Google's Jetpack suite of libraries for Android development.
what_you_will_learn: Benchmarking UI components — measuring the time it takes for views to render, update, and animate
Using BenchmarkRule — a simple way to define custom benchmark rules for specific use cases
Measuring frame rate and latency — understanding how your app's performance affects user experience
Profiling with Android Studio — using the built-in profiling tools to analyze benchmark results and identify performance issues
Optimizing UI rendering — techniques for reducing render times, such as lazy loading and view recycling
Using Jetpack Compose — a modern declarative UI framework that integrates seamlessly with Benchmark
Measuring memory usage — understanding how your app's memory consumption affects performance

knowledge.what_it_is: Firebase Distribution is a service provided by Google that allows Android developers to distribute their apps on the Google Play Store, while also providing features like app updates, crash reporting, and analytics.
what_you_will_learn: Google Play Console — uploading APKs, managing app listings, tracking performance metrics
App Distribution API — automating app releases, scheduling updates, and targeting specific users or devices
Firebase Crashlytics — collecting crash reports, identifying issues, and optimizing app stability
Firebase Performance Monitoring — monitoring app performance, identifying bottlenecks, and optimizing user experience
Google Play Store Optimization — improving app visibility, increasing downloads, and enhancing user engagement
App Bundle Format — reducing APK size, improving download times, and making updates more efficient
Dynamic Delivery — delivering different versions of the app based on device configuration, language, or other factors

knowledge.what_it_is: A digitally signed Android app package (APK) is a secure way to distribute apps on Google Play Store and other app stores, ensuring authenticity and preventing tampering. The signing process involves creating a digital certificate using tools like Java Keytool or Android Studio's built-in key management system.
what_you_will_learn: Keystore — a container for storing cryptographic keys used for signing APKs
Key alias — a unique identifier for each key, used to sign the APK
SHA-256 certificate fingerprint — a unique string representing the digital certificate, used in Google Play Store configuration
V1 and V2 signature schemes — the two types of digital signatures supported by Android, with V2 being more secure but also more complex to implement
JAR signer tool (jarsigner) — a command-line utility for signing APKs using Java-based certificates
Android Studio's built-in key management system — a graphical interface for creating and managing keys, certificates, and signatures
Google Play Store's APK signature verification process — how the store checks the integrity of uploaded APKs
Certificate revocation lists (CRL) and online certificate status protocol (OCSP) — mechanisms for checking the validity of digital certificates

knowledge.what_it_is: The Google Play Store is a digital distribution platform developed by Google, where Android developers can publish and distribute their apps to millions of users worldwide. It's a crucial channel for Android app monetization and user acquisition.
what_you_will_learn: Publishing process — understanding the steps involved in publishing an app on the Play Store, including creating a developer account, setting up the store listing, and uploading APKs

App signing — learning about the importance of code signing for Android apps, how to create and manage keystore files, and how to use tools like JAR Signer or Android Studio's built-in signing feature
Google Play Console — navigating the console's features, including app management, analytics, and monetization options
Play Store policies — understanding the guidelines and restrictions for publishing apps on the Play Store, including content ratings, in-app purchases, and advertising requirements
App optimization — learning how to optimize an app's visibility and performance on the Play Store, including using keywords, descriptions, and screenshots effectively
Google Play Services — integrating Google Play Services into an app, including Google Sign-In, Google Maps, and Google Cloud Messaging (GCM)
In-app purchases — implementing in-app purchase mechanisms using Google Play Billing Library or other third-party libraries
App updates — understanding how to manage and distribute app updates on the Play Store, including using Android Studio's built-in update feature or third-party tools like Fastlane
